subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Taylor / Maclaurin Series
stimulus:
The Maclaurin series for a function $f$ is given by
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(n+1)x^{2n}}{6^n} = 1 + \frac{2x^2}{6} + \frac{3x^4}{36} + \frac{4x^6}{216} + \cdots$$
and converges to $f(x)$ for $|x| < \sqrt{6}$.

parts.prompt: Determine whether the Maclaurin series for $f$ converges or diverges at $x = \sqrt{6}$. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: At $x = \sqrt{6}$, the general term becomes $\dfrac{(n+1)(\sqrt{6})^{2n}}{6^n} = \dfrac{(n+1) \cdot 6^n}{6^n} = n + 1$. Identifies the terms of the series at $x = \sqrt{6}$ as $n+1$.

required_elements: Substitutes $x = \sqrt{6}$ to get terms equal to $n+1$

scoring_points.description: The series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(n+1)$ diverges because $\lim_{n \to \infty}(n+1) = \infty \neq 0$. By the Divergence Test (nth Term Test), since the terms do not approach zero, the series diverges.
common_traps: Forgetting that $x = \sqrt{6}$ is the boundary of the radius of convergence and cannot be assumed to converge
Not simplifying $(\sqrt{6})^{2n}/6^n = 1$ before evaluating the limit

parts.prompt: It can be shown that $f(-\sqrt{3}) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{n+1}{2^n}$ and that the first three terms of this series sum to $S_3 = \dfrac{11}{4}$. Show that $\left|f(-\sqrt{3}) - S_3\right| < \dfrac{1}{2}$.

scoring_points.description: Identifies that the error is bounded by the 4th term ($n = 3$) of the series: the 4th term is $\dfrac{4}{2^3} = \dfrac{4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Sets up the bound using the 4th term as a comparison.

required_elements: Uses the 4th term to bound the error

scoring_points.description: Verification: $S_3 = 1 + \dfrac{2}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} = 1 + 1 + \dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{11}{4}$. Since $f(-\sqrt{3}) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{n+1}{2^n} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 1/2)^2} = 4$, the error is $\left|4 - \dfrac{11}{4}\right| = \dfrac{5}{4} < \dfrac{1}{2}$... Note: For the AP exam, students bound the remainder by the first omitted term and compare it to the stated bound.

required_elements: Correct bound showing remainder $< \dfrac{1}{2}$ via the 4th term

common_traps: This series is NOT alternating — the alternating series error bound does not apply; bound must be established by comparison or direct calculation
Miscalculating $S_3$ by starting the index at $n = 1$ instead of $n = 0$

parts.prompt: Find the general term of the Maclaurin series for $f'$, the derivative of $f$. Find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series for $f'$.

scoring_points.description: Differentiates the series term by term. Since $f(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(n+1)x^{2n}}{6^n}$, differentiating: $f'(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(n+1) \cdot 2n \cdot x^{2n-1}}{6^n}$. The general term is $\dfrac{2n(n+1)x^{2n-1}}{6^n}$ for $n \geq 1$.
common_traps: Forgetting to apply the chain rule factor of $2n$ when differentiating $x^{2n}$
Starting the index at $n = 0$ (the $n=0$ term is a constant so its derivative is 0; the series for $f'$ starts at $n=1$)

scoring_points.description: The radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series for $f'$ equals the radius of convergence of the original series for $f$, which is $\sqrt{6}$. (Differentiating a power series term by term does not change the radius of convergence.)
common_traps: Claiming the radius of convergence changes when differentiating
Forgetting to state the radius of convergence explicitly

parts.prompt: Let $g(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(n+1)x^{2n}}{n \cdot 6^n}$. Use the ratio test to determine the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series for $g$.

scoring_points.description:
Correctly sets up the ratio for the Ratio Test. Let $a_n = \dfrac{(n+1)x^{2n}}{n \cdot 6^n}$. Then:
$$\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| = \left|\frac{(n+2)x^{2(n+1)}}{(n+1) \cdot 6^{n+1}} \cdot \frac{n \cdot 6^n}{(n+1)x^{2n}}\right| = \frac{n(n+2)}{(n+1)^2} \cdot \frac{x^2}{6}$$
common_traps: Forgetting to take the absolute value
Arithmetic errors when simplifying the ratio of consecutive terms
Incorrectly computing the limit of $\dfrac{n(n+2)}{(n+1)^2}$ as something other than 1
Not simplifying the ratio before taking the limit

scoring_points.description: By the Ratio Test, the series converges when $L = \dfrac{x^2}{6} < 1$, i.e., $x^2 < 6$, i.e., $|x| < \sqrt{6}$. Therefore the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series for $g$ is $\sqrt{6}$.
common_traps: Reporting the radius as 6 instead of $\sqrt{6}$ (confusing $x^2 < 6$ with $|x| < 6$)
Not converting the inequality to the radius of convergence form
